titleStmt.title: Exponentially localised interface eigenmodes in finite chains of resonators

funder.orgName: Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council

analytic.title: Exponentially localised interface eigenmodes in finite chains of resonators

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

keywords.term: robust wave localisation AMS Subject classifications. 34L40

div.p: This paper studies wave localisation in chains of finitely many resonators. There is an extensive theory predicting the existence of localised modes induced by defects in infinitely periodic systems. This work extends these principles to finite-sized systems. We consider finite systems of subwavelength resonators arranged in dimers that have a geometric defect in the structure. This is a classical wave analogue of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model. We prove the existence of a spectral gap for defectless finite dimer structures and find a direct relationship between eigenvalues being within the spectral gap and the localisation of their associated eigenmode. Then we show the existence and uniqueness of an eigenvalue in the gap in the defect structure, proving the existence of a unique localised interface mode. To the best of our knowledge, our method, based on Chebyshev polynomials, is the first to characterise quantitatively the localised interface modes in systems of finitely many resonators.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Wave localisation at subwavelength scales has many important applications in nanophotonics and nanophononics [9,19,21]. Here, subwavelength means that the incident wavelengths are much larger than the size of the building blocks of the structure. When these relatively small building blocks are locally resonant (which, in the case studied here, will be due to large material contrasts) this allows for waves to be localised at subwavelength scales, thereby beating traditional diffraction limits [4,5,7]. This principle has unlocked a wealth of novel nanotechnologies [13,22].

In this paper, we consider wave localisation at the interface between two systems of finitely many subwavelength resonators. These resonators are arranged in pairs or dimers, such that the model we consider shares many of the features of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model in quantum mechanics [25]. We prove the existence of exponentially localised interface eigenmodes in this finite structure. These interface modes have been subject to numerous studies in the setting of infinite structures. A particular focus has been put on studying the topological properties of infinite periodic structures and then introducing carefully designed interfaces so as to create so-called topologically protected eigenmodes [8]. These modes have significant implications for applications since they are expected to be robust with respect to imperfections in the design. These concepts have been widely studied in a variety of settings, most notably in quantum mechanics for the Schrödinger operator [16,17] and more recently, for related continuous models of classical wave systems in [12,20,24,26,27]. In finite-sized systems, these eigenmodes have been observed both experimentally and numerically; see, for instance, [2,8,10,30] and references therein.

The present work considers the far less-explored but more realistic physical setting of interface modes in finite dimer structures. As far as we know, it is the first work to deal with the existence of interface modes in finite structures. It provides a one-to-one correspondence between the position of the eigenfrequency in the spectrum of the corresponding infinite periodic structure (i.e., in the asymptotic spectral bulk, its boundary or in the asymptotic spectral gap; see Definition 4.1) and the behaviour (localised versus delocalised) of the corresponding eigenmode. Our results hold for any finite and large enough system of dimer structure with defect satisfying a mild condition on the size of the resonators. Furthermore, we show that the eigenfrequencies lying in the gap converge exponentially as the size of the structure goes to infinity and provide an explicit and simple formula for the limit. We also show that the Hermitian nature of the system together with the position of the interface eigenfrequency in the gap yields a very strong stability property when the geometry of the system is perturbed.

Our approach is based on the capacitance matrix, which in general is a powerful tool for characterising the subwavelength eigenfrequencies of a system of high-contrast resonators [2,6,18]. In essence, the capacitance formulation provides a discrete approximation to the continuous spectral problem of the differential model, valid in the high-contrast asymptotic limit. This approximation is based solely on first principles and provides a natural starting point for both theoretical analysis and numerical simulation of wave localisation in the subwavelength regime [3,4,7]. In the case of our one-dimensional, finite system of dimer resonators with a geometric defect, the capacitance matrix is a perturbed tridiagonal block 2-Toeplitz matrix; see (2.6). Based on some properties of the Chebyshev polynomials, we prove existence and uniqueness of an eigenfrequency in the gap for the finite dimer structures with a geometric defect and show exponential localisation of the corresponding eigenmode. Our proof does not rely on any perturbation argument neither on any a prior assumption on the band gaps of the two dimer systems in the structure.

The paper is organised as follows. In Section 2, we introduce the capacitance matrix approximation of a finite, dimer system with a geometric defect in order to approximate its eigenfrequencies and associated eigenmodes. In Section 3, we first write the characteristic polynomial of the capacitance matrix associated to a defectless, finite dimer system in terms of Chebyshev polynomials. Then, we characterise the structure of the eigenvectors of the capacitance matrix associated with the perturbed dimer structure. Section 4 is devoted to the derivation of a direct relationship between an eigenvalue being within the spectral gap and the localisation of its corresponding eigenvector. In Section 5, we show the existence and uniqueness of an eigenvalue of the capacitance matrix lying in the asymptotic spectral gap and consequently the existence of a unique localised interface eigenvector. Furthermore, we show that this eigenvalue converges exponentially fast to a value in the gap as the system size increases. In Section 6, we analyse the robustness of the interface localisation with respect to imperfections in the structure design. The paper ends with some concluding remarks in Section 7. In Appendix A, we recall some basic definitions and results on pseudo-spectra of normal matrices. Appendix B is devoted to the discussion of the topological origin of the robustness of the interface eigenmodes.

div.head: One-dimensional subwavelength resonator systems
div.p: We consider a one-dimensional chain of N disjoint identical high-contrast resonators
div.formula: D i := (x L i , x R i ), where (x L,R i ) 1⩽i⩽N ⊂ R are the 2N boundaries satisfying x L i < x R i < x L i+1 for any 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N -1.
div.p: We fix the coordinates such that x L 1 = 0. We also denote by
div.formula: ℓ i = x R i -x L i
div.p: the length of the each of the resonators, and by s i = x L i+1 -x R i the spacing between the i-th and (i + 1)-th inclusions. The system is illustrated in Figure 1.
We use
div.formula: D := N i=1 (x L i , x R i ) x L 1 x R 1 ℓ 1 x L 2 x R 2 ℓ 2 x L 3 x R 3 ℓ 3 . . . x L 4 x R 4 ℓ 4 x L N -3 x R N -3 ℓ N -3 x L N -2 x R N -2 ℓ N -2 x L N -1 x R N -1 ℓ N -1 x L N x R N ℓ N s 1 s 2 s 3 s 4 s N -3 s N -2 s N -1 Figure 1.
div.p: A chain of N resonators, with lengths (ℓ i ) 1⩽i⩽N and spacings (s i ) 1⩽i⩽N -1 (which will be chosen to alternate between two distinct values, as depicted).
to denote the set of subwavelength resonators. In this paper, we only consider systems of identically sized resonators, that is
div.formula: ℓ i = ℓ ∈ R >0 for all 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N.
div.p: This will simplify the formulas in our subsequent analysis and is sufficient to observe the physical phenomena we are interested in.
In this work, we consider the one-dimensional wave equation propagating in a heterogeneous medium with space-dependent material parameters:
div.formula: ω 2 κ(x) u(x) + d dx 1 ρ(x) d dx u(x) = 0, x ∈ R . (2.1)
div.p: The material parameters κ(x) and ρ(x) are piecewise constant in the interior and exterior of the resonators
div.formula: κ(x) = κ b , x ∈ D, κ, x ∈ R \D, and ρ(x) = ρ b , x ∈ D, ρ, x ∈ R \D,
div.p: where the constants ρ b , ρ, κ, κ b ∈ R >0 . The wave speeds inside the set D of resonators and inside the background medium R \D, are denoted respectively by v b and v, the wave numbers respectively by k b and k, and the contrast between the densities of the resonators and the background medium by δ:
div.formula: v b := κ b ρ b , v := κ ρ , k b := ω v b , k := ω v , δ := ρ b ρ . (2.2)
div.p: For these step-wise defined material parameters, the wave problem determined by (2.1) reduces to the following system of coupled one-dimensional Helmholtz equations:
div.formula:                                          d 2 dx 2 u(x) + ω 2 v 2 u(x) = 0, x ∈ R \D, d 2 dx 2 u(x) + ω 2 v 2 b u(x) = 0, x ∈ D, u| R (x L,R i ) -u| L (x L,R i ) = 0, 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N, du dx R (x L i ) -δ du dx L (x L i ) = 0, 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N, δ du dx R (x R i ) - du dx L (x R i ) = 0, 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N, d d|x| -ik u = 0 for x ∈ (-∞, x L 1 ) ∪ (x R N , +∞),(2.3)
div.p: where for a one-dimensional function w we denote by w| L (x) := lim s→0 s>0
w(x -s) and w| R (x) := lim s→0 s>0
w(x + s)

if the limits exist. We are interested in the high-contrast regime characterised by the contrast parameter δ being small. In this case, there exist resonant modes of the system at subwavelength frequencies, for which the size of the resonators is smaller than the wavelength in the background medium.
Definition 2.1. A resonance ω(δ) ∈ C for which there exists a non-trivial solution to (2.3) is called subwavelength in the high-contrast regime if ω(δ) → 0 as δ → 0.

One consequence of this asymptotic ansatz is that it lends itself to characterisation using asymptotic analysis [6]. This limit recovers subwavelength resonances, while keeping the size of the resonators fixed.

In [18], an asymptotic analysis in the subwavelength limit was performed on the system of one-dimensional subwavelength resonators considered here. It was shown that the leadingorder behavior of the resonances is given by the eigenpairs of the capacitance matrix:
This is a modified version of the conventional capacitance matrix that is often used to characterise many-body low-frequency resonance problems. We summaries the findings of [18] here below.
Proposition 2.2. Consider a system of N subwavelength resonators with size ℓ and spacings
Assume that the eigenvalues of C are simple. Then, the N subwavelength resonant frequencies ω i of (2.3) satisfy to the first order
div.formula: ω i = v b δλ i + O(δ),
div.p: where (λ i ) 1⩽i⩽N are the eigenvalues of the eigenvalue problem
div.formula: Ca i = λ i ℓa i 1 ⩽ i ⩽ N. (2.5)
div.p: Furthermore, let u i (x) be a subwavelength eigenmode corresponding to ω i and let a i be the corresponding eigenvector of C. Then
div.formula: u i (x) = N j=1 a (j) i V j (x) + O(δ)
div.p: where a (j) i denotes the j-th entry of the eigenvector a i and V j (x) is piecewise linear, supported in (x R j-1 , x L j+1 ) and V j (x) = 1 for x ∈ (x L j , x R j ). Proposition 2.2 states that eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the capacitance matrix stand in a one-to-one correspondence with subwavelength resonant frequencies and modes of the associated system.

div.head: Dimer chains with a defect
div.p: Systems of repeated dimers (that is s i = s i-2 for 3 ⩽ i ⩽ N ) are of particular interest as the corresponding infinite structure can be studied with Floquet-Bloch band theory [2]; when the two repeating separation distances are distinct, this provides a non-trivial example of a band gap between the subwavelength spectral bands. Here, we consider systems of dimers with a defect in the geometric structure, so that at some point the repeating pattern of alternating separation distances is broken. This is inspired by the famous Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model from quantum settings and is the canonical example of a topologically protected interface mode [25]. It is a system of N = 4m + 1 for m ∈ N resonators such that
div.formula: s i = s i-2 for 3 ⩽ i ⩽ 2m, s i = s i+2 for 2m + 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 4m -2,
div.p: where we typically assume s 1 = s 2m+2 and s 2 = s 2m+1 so that the system is symmetric with respect to the center of the (2m + 1)-th resonator with spacings s 1 and s 2 . A sketch of this system with a defect is shown in Figure 2. For such systems the geometric symmetries mean that the capacitance matrix from (2.4) has the following tridiagonal block structure:
where
It will be useful to have notation for the top left (2m + 1) × (2m + 1)-submatrix C 1 and the bottom right (2m + 1) × (2m + 1)-submatrix C 2 (as highlighted by the shading in (2.6)).

Figure 3b shows the spectrum and the eigenvectors of the capacitance matrix (2.6). It is immediately clear that there exists a spectral gap and there exists a localised eigenmode associated to an eigenvalue in the gap.

div.head: Perturbed tridiagonal 2-Toeplitz matrices
div.p: In this section we will briefly recall results established in [3] about eigenvalues and eigenvectors of tridiagonal 2-Toeplitz matrices with perturbations on the corners.
Denote by and
the prototypical tridiagonal 2-Toeplitz matrices with perturbations on the corners.

div.head: Eigenvalues
div.p: We define the polynomials
where U k is the Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind, as well as the function
The following proposition holds. are respectively
div.formula: χ A (a,b) 2k+1 (x) = (x -α -a -b) P * k (x) + ab (x -α) -aβ 2 1 -bβ 2 2 P * k-1 (x) (3.5) and χ A (a,b) 2k (x) = P * k (x) + (a + b) (α -x) + ab + β 2 2 P * k-1 (x) + abβ 2 1 P * k-2 (x) . (3.6)

div.head: Eigenvectors
div.p: We start by defining two families of polynomials p (ξp,ξq) k+1 (x) and q (ξp,ξq) k+1 (x) as solutions to the recursion relations p (ξp,ξq) 0
div.formula: (µ) = ξ p , p (ξp,ξq) 1 (µ) = 2µξ p + ξ p -ξ q β , p (ξp,ξq) k+1 (µ) = 2µ p (ξp,ξq) k (µ) -p (ξp,ξq) k-1 (µ),(3.7)
div.p: and q (ξp,ξq) 0
(µ) = ξ q , q (ξp,ξq) 1
div.formula: (µ) = (2µ + β)ξ p + ξ p -ξ q β , q (ξp,ξq) k+1 (µ) = 2µ q (ξp,ξq) k (µ) -q (ξp,ξq) k-1 (µ),(3.8)
div.p: where β = β 2 /β 1 . Then we state the following result.
div.formula: Proposition 3.2. Let λ be an eigenvalue of A (a,b) 2k+1 (α, β 1 , β 2 ).
div.p: Then, using µ := y(λ) where y is as in (3.4), the eigenvector corresponding to λ is given by
div.formula: x = q (ξp,ξq) 0 (µ) , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) 0 (µ) , q (ξp,ξq) 1 (µ) , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) 1 (µ) , . . . , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) k-1 (µ) , q (ξp,ξq) k (µ) . (3.9) If λ is an eigenvalue of A (a,b) 2k (α, β 1 , β 2 )
div.p: , then the corresponding eigenvector is given by
div.formula: x = q (ξp,ξq) 0 (µ) , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) 0 (µ) , q (ξp,ξq) 1 (µ) , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) 1 (µ) , . . . , - 1 β 1 (α -λ) p (ξp,ξq) k-1 (µ) . (3.10) In both cases, ξ q = (α -λ), ξ p = (α + a -λ).
div.p: The last result of this subsection is on the structure of the eigenvectors for the capacitance matrix C defined by (2.6). Proposition 3.3 is an adapted version of [3,Theorem 4.3]. Proposition 3.3. Let (λ, v) be an eigenpair of C and let µ := y(λ). Then v is given by v = (x (1) , x (2) , . . . , x (2m) , x (2m+1) , (-1) σ x (2m) , . . . , (-1) σ x (2) , (-1) σ x (1) ) ⊤ , (3.11) where x ∈ R 2m+1 is as in (3.9) with ξ q = (α -λ), ξ p = (α + a -λ) and σ ∈ {0, 1} except for x ∈ span{1} where σ = 1.
Proof. We will show (3.11) by showing that (C -λI)v = 0 in two steps.
Step 1. In the first step, we consider the first 2m + 1 rows of (C -λI)v:
where we put * instead of x (2m) to show that we will not use this entry in what follows as we only need the first 2m rows of (3.12) to determine the 2m + 1 entries of x. We write x as
Considering the first row of (3.12), we can choose
Then by the second row, we have
which gives
The third row is
and thus,
Continuing the process, we can easily verify that
) it is now easy to see that x is of the form (3.9).

Step 2. We conclude the proof by considering the last 2m rows. By the fact that C is S = antidiag(1, . . . , 1) ∈ R (4m+1)×(4m+1) symmetric, i.e., SCS = C, and C has only simple eigenvalues [18] we can immediately conclude that Sv = ±v and thus obtain the desired result. ■

div.head: Asymptotic spectral gap and localised interface modes
div.p: In this section we will prove the existence of a spectral gap for a defectless structure of dimers. Furthermore, we will demonstrate that a direct relationship exists between an eigenvalue being within the spectral gap and the localisation of its corresponding eigenvector.

div.head: Asymptotic spectral gap
div.p: We first show the existence of a spectral gap for the capacitance matrix of an unperturbed structure of dimers.

By physical considerations we assume β 1 , β 2 < 0 and β 1 < β 2 . It will often be useful to shift C in order to have most diagonal entries zero, to that end we introduce z(x) := x -α. Definition 4.1 (Spectral bulk and gaps). Consider a finite structure of resonators. We define the asymptotic spectral bulk Σ and asymptotic spectral gap Γ of the structure as the spectral bulk and spectral gap (also known as band gap) of the associated infinite periodic system, respectively.

The spectral gap and spectral bulk of infinite periodic dimer systems have been computed in [2, Lemma 5.3]. Proposition 4.2. Consider a system of repeated dimers (without defect) with N = 2m resonators. Denote by C N the associated capacitance matrix and let Σ be the asymptotic spectral bulk. Then
where lim denotes the Hausdorff limit. Consequently, the asymptotic spectral gap is
Proof. From (3.6) we see that any eigenvalue λ of the capacitance matrix of a structure of dimer without defect -that is, a capacitance matrix of the form C N = A (β2,β2) 2m
-satisfy
, where for ease of notation we use z for z(λ) and y for y(z(λ)). Using the recurrence relation of the Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind we get that
So the eigenvalues are given by
div.formula: λ = α + (β 1 + β 2 ), λ = α + (β 2 -β 1 ) and λ = α ± β 2 1 + 2β 1 β 2 cos kπ m + β 2 2 , (4.1)
div.p: for 1 ⩽ k ⩽ m -1. The sought result follows now directly from inserting the definition of α, β 1 , β 2 in terms of s 1 and s 2 as in (2.7). ■
We will refer to spectral bulk and spectral gap of the physical system and of the capacitance matrix interchangeably, using the identification Proposition 2.2.

div.head: Localised interface modes
div.p: In this subsection, we will prove that an eigenvalue in the gap is associated with an eigenvector that is exponentially localised in the sense that it decays exponentially away from the interface in both directions, within the limits of the finite structure. Definition 4.3 (Localised interface mode). Let v(x) be an eigenmode. Then we say that v is a localised interface mode at the point x 0 , if both |v(x-x 0 )| for x 0 < x ∈ D and |v(x 0 -x)| for x 0 > x ∈ D decay exponentially as a function of x ∈ D. The same terminology applies to the corresponding eigenvector of the capacitance matrix. Proposition 4.4 (Eigenvectors of C). Let C ∈ R 4m+1×4m+1 be the capacitance matrix of a defect structure as illustrated in Figure 2 and let (λ, v) be an eigenpair of C. Then, there exists |r| ⩾ 1 independent of m and A, B, A, B ∈ R dependent on m such that if y(λ) 2 > 1:
div.formula: v (|2m-2j|) = Ar j + Br -j , v (|2m-2j-1|) = Ar j + Br -j ; with A = r 1-m (c1r-c2) r 2 -1 = O( 1 r m ) and B = r m (c2r-c1) r 2 -1 = O(r m-1 ) as m → ∞ for c 1 , c 2 ∈ R independent of m.
div.p: The same asymptotics (with a slight different formula) hold for A and B; if y(λ) 2 < 1:
div.formula: v (|2m-2j|) = A cos(jθ) + B sin(jθ), v (|2m-2j-1|) = A cos(jθ) + B sin(jθ), with r = e iθ and A, B, A, B bounded as m → ∞; if y(λ) 2 = 1: r = ±1 and v (|2m-2j|) = Ar j 1 + Br j 1 • j, v (|2m-2j-1|) = Ar j 1 + Br j 1 • j, with A = r 1-m (c1mr-c1r-c2m) mr 2 -m-r 2 and B = r m (c2r-c1) mr 2 -m-r 2 as m → ∞ for c 1 , c 2 ∈ R inde- pendent of m.

div.head: The same asymptotics (with a slight different formula) hold for A and B.
div.p: Proof. It is enough to consider the p (ξp,ξq) k and q (ξp,ξq) k part of the eigenvector in Proposition 3.2 as the rest is a multiplicative factor. We only consider p (ξp,ξq) k as the procedure for q (ξp,ξq) k is exactly the same. Let (λ, v) be an eigenpair and recall from (3.7) the recurrence formula p (ξp,ξq)
where µ = y(λ). This is a two term recurrence formula with characteristic equation
having none, one or two roots r 1 and r 2 in exactly the cases delineated in the proposition.

The formulas for the eigenvectors follow. By Vieta's formula, we know that the two roots of (4.2) satisfy r 1 r 2 = 1 so one of them must satisfy |r i | ⩾ 1. The constants A, B, A, B ∈ R follow from the initial conditions (3.7) with the values expressed in Proposition 3.
The eigenvector in the case when y(λ) 2 > 1 is exponentially localized in the interface, as we can rescale the eigenvector to make
div.formula: v (|2m-2j|) = Br -j + Ar j , v (|2m-2j-1|) = Br -j + Ar j , where O(B) = O( B) = O(1) and O(Ar j ) = O( Ar j ) = o( 1 r m-1 ), j = 1, • • • , 2m
div.p: . We remark that the three cases presented in Proposition 4.4 correspond to λ ∈ Γ, λ ∈ Σ and λ ∈ ∂Σ, respectively. We show this behaviour in Figure 3. (a) Eigenvector associated to an eigenvalue in the asymptotic spectral bulk. In order to show that a localised eigenvector exists, it is thus sufficient and necessary to prove the existence of an eigenvalue in the asymptotic spectral gap. We will do this in the next section.

div.head: Existence, uniqueness, and convergence of the eigenvalue in the gap
div.p: In this section we will prove the existence of an unique eigenvalue in the gap for the defect structure and consequently the existence of a unique localised interface eigenvector. Furthermore, we analyse the behaviour as the size of the system grows, specifically the limiting behaviour as N → ∞. We will show that the eigenvalue of C lying in the asymptotic spectral gap converges exponentially fast to a value in the gap.

div.head: Existence
div.p: We first show the existence of the eigenvalue in the band gap. We start by remarking that by performing Laplace expansion on the top block of rows 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 2m + 1, we obtain that the determinant of C ∈ R (4m+1)×(4m+1) is given by
div.formula: det C = det A (a,η-α) 2m+1 (α, β 1 , β 2 ) • det A (0,b) 2m (α, β 1 , β 2 ) -β 2 2 det A (a,0) 2m (α, β 1 , β 2 ) • det A (0,b) 2m-1 (α, β 2 , β 1 ),(5.1)
div.p: where a = b = β 2 . We observe that the last term has β 1 and β 2 switched. Consequently, the characteristic polynomial p(x) of C is given by
as one swiftly remarks that
2m (α, β 1 , β 2 ) are similar matrices. For the sake of brevity, we rewrite
div.formula: p(x) = χ A (a,0) 2m (α,β1,β2) (x) AP * m (x) + BP * m-1 (x) -β 2 2 EP * m-1 (x) + F P * m-2 (x) ,
div.p: where A, B, E, F do not depend on m. Thus, λ ∈ Γ is an eigenvalue if and only if
div.formula: AP * m (λ) + BP * m-1 (λ) = β 2 2 EP * m-1 (λ) + F P * m-2 (λ) (5.3) ⇔ P * m (λ) A + B P * m-1 (λ) P * m (λ) = β 2 2 P * m-1 (λ) E + F P * m-2 (λ) P * m-1 (λ)
div.p: In the above step we were able to divide by χ A (a,0) 2m (λ) and P * m-2 (λ) as we only consider λ ∈ Γ so the latter term is non-zero because the Chebyshev polynomials only have roots in (-1, 1) and the former term was shown to be non-zero in Γ in Proposition 5.3. Moreover, since
div.formula: U m (y) = y + y 2 -1 m+1 -y -y 2 -1 m+1 2 y 2 -1 , the limit L(λ) := lim m→∞ P * m-1 (λ) P * m (λ) exists for all λ ∈ R. Then, [A + BL] = β 2 2 L [E + F L] ⇔ L 2 (β 2 2 F ) + L(β 2 2 E -B) -A = 0, from which we get the condition L(λ) = B -Eβ 2 2 ± 4AF β 2 2 + B 2 -2BEβ 2 2 + E 2 β 4 2 2F β 2 2 .
div.p: On the other hand, by the recurrence formula of U m , for λ ∈ Γ where y(z(λ)) < -1,
, (5.6) by using again the shorthand z for z(λ) and y for y(z(λ)). Now, an algebraic manipulation shows that conditions (5.5) and (5.6) have exactly one common solution λ 0 ∈ Γ given by

Proposition 5.1. Consider a perturbed structure of dimers as illustrated in Figure 2. For N large enough there exists at least one localised interface eigenvector of C with eigenvalue λ (N ) i in the band gap Γ.
Proof. Denote by
div.formula: f m (λ) := A + B P * m-1 (λ) P * m (λ) -β 2 2 P * m-1 (λ) P * m (λ) E + F P * m-2 (λ) P * m-1 (λ) , f ∞ := [A + BL(λ)] -β 2 2 L(λ) [E + F L(λ)] ,
div.p: and note that f ∞ (λ) = lim m→∞ f m (λ). By (5.7) there exists a λ 0 in the band gap such that f ∞ (λ 0 ) = 0. Furthermore, by the above formula of f ∞ (λ), we have
div.formula: f ∞ (λ 0 -ζ)f ∞ (λ 0 +ζ) < 0 for some ζ > 0 satisfying [λ 0 -ζ, λ 0 + ζ] ⊂ Γ.
div.p: By the sign-preserving property, we have f m (λ 0 -ζ)f m (λ 0 + ζ) < 0 for large enough m, which proves the existence of roots of f m (λ) in the band gap Γ. By Proposition 4.4 the corresponding eigenvector is a localised interface eigenvector. ■

div.head: Uniqueness
div.p: We now show the uniqueness of the eigenvalue in the band gap. We will use the following result about the monotonicity of Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind.
div.formula: Lemma 5.2. Let k ∈ N, then U k-1 (x) U k (x) is strictly decreasing for x ∈ (-∞, -1) ∪ (1, +∞) for any k ∈ N. Proof. Considering the derivative of U k-1 (x) U k (x)
div.p: , we have
A well-known property of Chebyshev polynomials is that
where T k (x) are the Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind. Thus, to demonstrate the negativity of (5.8) for |x| > 1, we only need to show that
It is also well-known that
div.formula: T ℓ (x)U n (x) = 1 2 (U ℓ+n (x) + U n-ℓ (x)) , if n ⩾ ℓ -1, 1 2 (U ℓ+n (x) -U ℓ-n-2 (x)) , if n ⩽ ℓ -2.
div.p: So that (5.9) becomes

) and the proof is complete. ■ Proposition 5.3. There exists at most one eigenvalue of C as defined in (2.6) lying in the asymptotic spectral gap Γ = (2/s 2 , 2/s 1 ). In particular, for m large enough, there exists exactly one eigenvalue in Γ.
Proof. Considering the compression of C obtained by removing the central row and column we obtain a block diagonal matrix with blocks given by
and P BP,

where P = antidiag(1, . . . , 1). Remark that the same assumptions as stated before Definition 4.1 on the coefficients of B hold, except that now b = 0, that is, the matrix B is of the type A (a,0) 2m and its characteristic polynomials is thus given by
. Consequently, we see from (3.3) that λ is an eigenvalue of B if and only if

by using again the shorthand z for z(λ) and y for y(z(λ)). Remark that L(z) in (5.11) is strictly monotonous increasing with a pole of order one at β 2 . Furthermore, by Lemma 5.2 R(z) is strictly monotonically increasing on (β 1 -β 2 , 0) and strictly monotonically decreasing on (0,
We first consider the case 2β 2 < β 1 < β 2 , which equates to the pole of L(z) lying outside of the gap Γ. In this case we have
div.formula: L(β 1 -β 2 ) = - β 1 2β 2 -β 1 < - m m + 1 = R(β 1 -β 2 ), L(β 2 -β 1 ) = -1 < - m m + 1 = R(β 2 -β 1 ), so that L(z) < R(z) for all z ∈ (β 1 -β 2 , β 2 -β 1 ). The case β 1 ⩽ 2β 2 is similar. The pole z = β 2 is now in the interval of interest, but now L(z) > 0 > R(z) for z ∈ (β 1 -β 2 , β 2 ) and R(z) < L(z) for z ∈ (β 2 , β 2 -β 1
div.p: ) by the same argument as the one in the previous case. Thus there can be at most one eigenvalue in the gap. For m large enough, by Proposition 5.1 there exists exactly one eigenvalue in the gap. ■ Remark 5.4. We remark that if one can show the existence of an eigenvector in the band gap Γ for the capacitance matrix C with general size m, then by Proposition 5.3, it is unique.

div.head: Convergence
div.p: Last, we show the exponential convergence of the resonant frequency in the gap and conclude the section by the following theorem.
exponentially as N → ∞. In particular, for N big enough,
for some A, B > 0 independent of N .

Proof. The limit λ i can be computed from (5.7). The only part left to prove is the convergence rate. Denote by 0 d ∈ R d the zero element of that vector space and by v i,N the l 2 -normalised localised eigenvector associated to λ (N ) i
. Then, we remark that for some C 1 > 0. So if v i,N needs to be normalised v
i,N decays exponentially. By (5.14), ε N decays exponentially, which together with Proposition 2.2 proves (5.13).

We remark that, combined with Proposition 4.4, Theorem 5.5 also gives the decaying rate of the interface mode for a structure with sufficiently many resonators. We display the left-hand side of (5.13) for a structure with s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 2.

div.head: Stability analysis
div.p: Interface modes of SSH-like structures are well known to be stable, i.e., perturbations of the system affect them only slightly. this section we show that perturbation in the geometry have limited effect on both the resonant frequencies and the associated modes and we quantify this effect.
To this end we consider a system of N = 4m + 1 resonators as shown in Figure 2 but the spacings s i are now perturbed:
div.formula: s i =          s 1 + ε i , 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 2m, i odd, s 2 + ε i , 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 2m, i even, s 1 + ε i , 2m + 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 4m, i even, s 2 + ε i , 2m + 1 ⩽ i ⩽ 4m, i odd. (6.1)
div.p: Furthermore, we denote
The following proposition handles stability of the eigenvalues and is a direct application of the well-known Weyl theorem.
Proposition 6.1. Let C be the capacitance matrix associated to the structure described in (6.1) and let
Then, the eigenvalues λ k (sorted increasingly) satisfy
where λ k are the eigenvalues of C.

Proof. Weyl's theorem states the same result but with the bound of (6.4) replaced by ∥ C-C∥. However, for a tridiagonal matrix M with 1 in its kernel, ∥M ∥ ⩽ 2 max i |M i(i-1) | + |M i(i+1) | by the Gershgorin circle theorem so we obtain the result. ■ Applying Proposition 6.1 to our system of dimers where the perturbations | ε i | ⩽ η are in the interval (-η, η) for some η > 0, we obtain that the eigenvalue perturbation is bounded by

In order to analyse the stability of the eigenvectors, we will use the Davis-Kahan theorem [14], which needs some preliminary introduction. Let E and F be d × r matrices with orthonormal columns such that span(E) = E and span(F ) = F. The canonical angles between E and F are defined as
div.formula: θ j = cos -1 σ j 1 ⩽ j ⩽ r,
div.p: where σ j are the r singular values of E T F . We denote by Θ(E, F) = diag(θ 1 , . . . , θ r ) the canonical angles' matrix.
The Davis-Kahan theorem states the following. Theorem 6.2. Let S and S be two d × d symmetric matrices with eigenvalues
respectively. Fix 1 ⩽ r ⩽ s ⩽ d and let V and V be the matrices having as columns the eigenvectors corresponding to λ j and λ j for r ⩽ j ⩽ s. Let span(V ) = V and span( V ) = V. Define
div.formula: δ := inf{|λ -λ| : λ ∈ [λ s , λ r ], λ ∈ (-∞, λ s+1 ] ∪ [ λ r-1 , ∞)}. If δ > 0, then ∥ sin Θ(V, V)∥ 2 ⩽ ∥S -S∥ 2 δ , where sin Θ(V, V) ii = sin(Θ(V, V) ii ).
div.p: As a direct consequence of Theorem 6.2, we have the following theorem for the stability of the interface eigenmodes. (6.3). Let v and v be the eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalues λ i and λ i in the gap of C and C, respectively. Then
div.formula: Theorem 6.3. Let ε < 1 2 1 s1 -1 s2 in
where δ := min{|λ i -λ i+1 |, |λ i -λ i-1 |} and δ 0 = min{|λ i -λ i+1 |, |λ i -λ i-1 |}.
div.p: The a priori estimate (6.6) holds for δ 0 > 2ε.
Remark that for s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 2 and m large we have δ 0 ≈ 0.219 so that the a priori estimate (6.6) holds for ε < 0.1. This a priori estimate is, however, suboptimal as Figure 6b shows.
Proof of Theorem 6.3. The proof immediately follows from Theorem 6.2 with r = s by using the bound ∥v -v∥ 2 ⩽ √ 2 sin Θ(v, v) [29]. ■

In Figure 6 we show numerically the high stability of the interface modes. We consider perturbations of the type ε i ∼ U (-η, η) where we call η the perturbation size, we display the latter as percentage relative to the resonator's size. Figure 6a shows that the interface eigenfrequency (lying in the gap) is only minimally perturbed even by perturbation in the size of 20%. We remark that numerically the bound of (6.4) can even be sharpened to 6b shows ∥v -v∥ 2 for various perturbation sizes and normalised v and v. The black lines shows the average over 10 4 runs while the gray area encloses the range from the minimum to the maximum value of ∥v -v∥ 2 .

div.head: Concluding remarks
div.p: In this paper, we have quantitatively characterised interface eigenmodes in finite, dimer systems of subwavelength resonators with a geometric defect and proved the exponential decay of their associated eigenmodes. Our characterisation is based on (broken) translation invariance properties of the associated capacitance matrix together with properties of Chebyshev polynomials. The 3-term recurrence relation satisfied by the Chebyshev polynomials is shown to be useful for analysing spectra of tridiagonal (perturbed) 2-Toeplitz matrices.

Following this line of research, it would be very interesting to generalise the results obtained in this paper to extended (known also as multi-band) SSH models, exhibiting exponentially localised interface eigenmodes with corresponding eigenfrequencies inside the multiple subwavelength band gaps of the structure [23,28]. Another highly interesting direction would be to extend the current results to finite dimer systems of three-dimensional subwavelength resonators. In the case of three-dimensional systems of subwavelength resonators, the main difficulty occurs from the long-range interactions between the resonators, which lead to a slow decay of the off-diagonal terms of the corresponding capacitance matrix. Nevertheless, in view of the recent results on k-banded approximations of three-dimensional capacitance matrices [1] together with the convergence spectral results as the size of the structure goes to infinity in [3,7], it may be possible to prove existence and uniqueness of localised eigenmodes in finite chains of subwavelength resonators in three dimensions, as numerically shown in [8]. Another very interesting problem is to relate the localisation effect at the interface to the statistics of the eigenvalues of the capacitance matrix under random perturbations in the parameters of the system. This would extend the well-known Thouless localisation criterion [15] to interface eigenmodes.

In Figure 7 we show the values of J d (v) for two structures composed of 40 dimers. Figure 7a shows the case s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 2 while Figure 7b shows the case s 1 = 2 and s 2 = 1. We observe a very different behaviour, but in particular the value of interest in view of (B.2) is the one at λ = 1, where the two structures take values ≈ +1 and ≈ -1 respectively showing that through (B.4) we may generalise (B.2) to discrete structures.

figure.head: Figure 2 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 2. Dimer structure with a defect.

Eigenvalues of(2.6). As red cross a specific eigenvalue lying isolated from the others. A selection of eigenvectors of C from (2.6).All eigenvectors are superimposed and with unit norm. The red solid eigenvector corresponds to the red cross eigenvalue in (3a).

figure.head: Figure 3 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 3. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the capacitance matrix (2.6) for N = 41, s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 3.

figure.head: Proposition 3 . 1 (
figure.figDesc: Eigenvalues). The characteristic polynomials of A(a,b) 2k+1 and A (a,b) 2k
Applying to(3.14) the same manipulations as those used in the proof of[3, Theorem 3.3],

figure.head: v 1 |v
figure.figDesc: (2m-2j-1) v (2m-2j) (b) Eigenvector associated to an eigenvalue in the boundary of the asymptotic spectral bulk. (2m-2j-1) | |v (2m-2j) | (c) Eigenvector associated to an eigenvalue in the asymptotic spectral gap. y axis in log-scale.

figure.head: Figure 4 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 4. Eigenvector behaviour based on the location of eigenvalue. Computation performed with N = 81, s 1 = 1, s 2 = 3.

figure.head: Theorem 5 . 5 .
figure.figDesc: Consider a perturbed structure of dimers as illustrated in Figure2. For N large enough there exists a unique interface mode with eigenfrequency ω

figure.head: i,N ) 2 =
figure.figDesc: : ε N .(5.14) Remark also that the same estimation holds if we replace C N +4 in (5.14) with C N +4k for any k ∈ N. This shows that λ(N ) i is an ε-pseudo-eigenvalue of C N +4k for any k ∈ N for every ε > ε N . Since C N is normal, by Theorem A.2, we have |λ i -λ (N ) i | ⩽ ε N .On the other hand, for N big enough, λ (N ) i is close to λ i and the formulae of Proposition 4.4 yield v (1) i,N = O(1) ∥v i,N ∥ 2 = O(e C1N ) as N → ∞ (5.15)

figure.head: Figure 5 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 5. Convergence of the eigenvalue in the gap (y-axis in log scale).We display the left-hand side of (5.13) for a structure with s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 2.

Spectrum of the capacitance matrix with perturbations given by εi ∼ U (-η, η). For every perturbation size the spectrum of one realisation is shown. The eigenvalue in red corresponds to the localised interface mode. Stability of the interface mode. The solid black line shows the average dislocation over 10 4 runs, while the gray area encloses the range from the minimum to the maximum dislocation observed over these realisations.

figure.head: Figure 6 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 6. The interface eigenvalue and the corresponding interface mode are very stable also in presence of big perturbations. Simulations in a system of N = 41 resonators with s 1 = 1 and s 2 = 2.Perturbations are uniformly distributed in (-η, η) where we call η the perturbation size, expressed relatively to the resonators' sizes.
Discrete indicator J d (v) s1 = 1 and s2 = 2. Discrete indicator J d (v) s1 = 2 and s2 = 1.

figure.head: Figure 7 .
figure.figDesc: Figure 7. The discrete indicator function of (B.4) have very different behaviour for s 1 < s 2 and s 1 > s 2 . The values at the end of the bands approximate accurately the values of the infinite periodic case of (B.2).

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: This work was supported by Swiss National Science Foundation grant number 200021-200307 and by the Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) under grant number EP/X027422/1.

div.head: Data Availability
div.p: The data that support the findings of this study are openly available at https://zenodo. org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10361315.

div.head: Conflict of interest
div.p: The authors have no competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article.

div.head: Appendix A. Pseudo-spectrum of a normal matrix
div.p: Definition A.1. σ ε (A) is the set of z ∈ C such that ∥(z -A)v∥ < ε, for some v ∈ C N with ∥v∥ = 1.
The next theorem expresses these facts with the aid of the following notation for an open ε-ball:
In this theorem, a sum of sets has the usual meaning:

div.head: Appendix B. Topological origin
div.p: Infinite SSH structures have long been known for their topological nature. In this section, we show that a topological invariant can be defined also for finite structures in such a way that when the size of the system grows they converge to the infinite invariants. For onedimensional crystals, the Zak phase has been shown to be related through an if and only if condition to the existence of interface modes, see e.g. [11,Theorem 1] and also [17,20]. Denoting by u α j a family of eigenmodes depending piecewise smoothly on the quasiperiodicity α, the Zak phase is defined as

where Y * denotes the first Brillouin zone and ⟨•, •⟩ the usual L 2 inner product. The Zak phase is also related to the symmetries of the eigenfunctions [11]. Specifically, denoting by u + j the eigenfunction associated to the quasifrequency that maximises the j-th band function ω α j we may define bulk topological index of the j-th band gap as
for j = 1, 2, where P denotes the mirroring of the unit cell Y about its center. Then, the Zak phase is related to J j via
Previous work [2, Proposition 5.5] has shown that the Zak phase of a periodic system of dimers is quantised and depends on the inter-and intra-spacing between the cells. In particular,
and thus
We will show now that (B.2) lends itself well to a reformulation in the finite case. We denote by P d the discrete equivalent of P that is, for v ∈ R 2k ,
and define the discrete equivalent of (B.2) by

Locally Resonant Sonic Materials
